reasoning: The Lean formalization accurately captures the informal claim. S(α) is defined as {x : ℕ | ∃ n ≥ 1, x = Nat.floor(nα)}, which for α > 0 and n ≥ 1 coincides with {⌊nα⌋ : n = 1, 2, 3, ...} (Nat.floor agrees with the standard floor on positive reals). The statement Set.Ici 1 = {x : ℕ | 1 ≤ x} = {1, 2, 3, ...} in ℕ matches the informal {1, 2, 3, ...}. The three pairwise intersection conditions together with the union equation correctly encode "disjoint union of three sets". The negated existential ¬∃ α β γ > 0 faithfully captures "cannot be expressed". Allowing α ∈ (0, 1) rather than α ≥ 1 is logically equivalent because for α < 1 we have 0 ∈ S(α), which automatically breaks the union equation (since 0 ∉ Set.Ici 1), so the broader quantification is vacuous in those cases.
